facts.text: Michael Phelps holds the all-time record with 28 Olympic medals — 23 gold, 3 silver, and 2 bronze.
An Olympic swimming pool is exactly 50 meters long, 25 meters wide, and at least 2 meters deep.
The butterfly stroke was originally developed as a variation of breaststroke in the 1930s before becoming its own official event.
Greg Louganis hit his head on the springboard during the 1988 Seoul Olympics preliminary round, then returned to win gold in both springboard and platform events.
Captain Matthew Webb became the first person to swim the English Channel on August 25, 1875, completing the crossing in about 21 hours and 45 minutes.
Freestyle is not actually a specific stroke — it means swimmers can use any stroke, but the front crawl is universally chosen because it is the fastest.
Swim goggles were not commonly used in competitive swimming until the 1970s. Before that, swimmers simply endured the chlorine.
Katie Ledecky's dominance in distance freestyle is so complete that she has lapped competitors in 1500m races.
Water polo became an Olympic sport in 1900 at the Paris Games, making it one of the oldest team sports in the Olympics.
The backstroke is the only competitive swimming stroke that starts in the water rather than from a diving block.
Johnny Weissmuller, who won five Olympic gold medals in swimming in the 1920s, went on to become the most famous movie Tarzan.
Tech suits — full-body polyurethane swimsuits — were banned by FINA (now World Aquatics) in 2010 after a spate of world records that were deemed equipment-aided.
Tom Daley won his first World Championship diving title at age 15, making him one of the youngest world champions in the sport.
The individual medley (IM) requires swimmers to complete all four strokes in a specific order: butterfly, backstroke, breaststroke, then freestyle.
Marathon swimming covers a distance of 10 kilometers in open water and has been an Olympic event since 2008.
Mark Spitz set a then-record of seven gold medals at a single Olympics at the 1972 Munich Games, each in world-record time.
The ancient Greeks and Romans considered swimming an essential life skill, and Roman bathhouses often included swimming areas.
A regulation diving platform stands 10 meters above the water — roughly the height of a three-story building.
Caeleb Dressel won five gold medals at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, excelling in both individual and relay sprint events.
Pull buoys are foam floats placed between a swimmer's thighs to isolate the upper body and improve arm stroke technique.
Synchronized swimming was officially renamed 'artistic swimming' by FINA in 2017.
Ian Thorpe, nicknamed 'Thorpedo,' became the youngest male to represent Australia in any sport when he competed at age 14.
The world's deepest swimming pool, Deep Dive Dubai, plunges 60 meters (nearly 200 feet) and holds 14 million liters of water.
Breaststroke is the oldest known swimming stroke, with depictions found in Stone Age cave paintings over 7,000 years old.
Michael Phelps's wingspan of 6 feet 7 inches exceeds his height of 6 feet 4 inches, giving him a natural hydrodynamic advantage.
Springboard diving uses a flexible board set at 1 meter or 3 meters above the water, allowing divers to generate height through the board's spring.
Open water swimmers often grease their bodies with petroleum jelly or lanolin to reduce chafing and provide slight insulation against cold water.
Dawn Fraser of Australia was the first swimmer to break the 1-minute barrier in the 100m freestyle, clocking 59.9 seconds in 1962.
The first modern Olympic swimming events took place in open water at the Bay of Zea near Piraeus, Greece, in 1896.
Interval training in swimming typically involves repeated sets of a set distance with controlled rest periods, such as 10 × 100m on 1:30.
Esther Williams, known as 'America's Mermaid,' turned her swimming talent into a Hollywood career starring in elaborate aquatic musicals in the 1940s and 1950s.
Water polo players tread water for the entire game and are not allowed to touch the bottom of the pool or hang on the wall.
Swim caps reduce drag and keep hair out of a swimmer's face, but they actually do very little to keep hair dry.
The 'Speedo LZR Racer' suit, introduced in 2008, was so effective that 94% of swimming gold medalists at the Beijing Olympics wore one.
Alexander Popov of Russia dominated the 50m and 100m freestyle in the 1990s, winning gold in both events at the 1992 and 1996 Olympics.
In diving, a 'rip entry' is the term for a near-splashless entry into the water — the ultimate goal of every competitive diver.
The English Channel is about 21 miles (34 km) at its narrowest point, but strong currents mean swimmers actually cover much greater distances.
Tapering is a training strategy where swimmers reduce their training volume by 40-60% in the weeks before a major competition to allow full recovery and peak performance.
Katie Ledecky won her first Olympic gold medal at age 15 in the 800m freestyle at the 2012 London Olympics.
The wave pool was first patented in 1927, but commercially viable wave pools didn't appear until the 1960s.
In artistic (synchronized) swimming, athletes are not allowed to touch the bottom of the pool during their routine.
Kickboards isolate the legs during swim training, helping swimmers develop stronger kicks and better body position.
Competitive swimming pools use touchpads at each end that record times to the hundredth of a second when a swimmer's hand or body contacts them.
Michael Phelps consumed an estimated 8,000 to 10,000 calories per day during peak training.
The 'dolphin kick' used in butterfly and off walls mimics a dolphin's tail motion, with both legs moving together in an undulating wave.
Hungary has been a dominant force in Olympic water polo, winning more gold medals in the sport than any other nation.
Gertrude Ederle became the first woman to swim the English Channel in 1926, beating the existing men's record by nearly two hours.
The first indoor swimming pool in the world was built in London in 1828, called St George's Baths.
Fins are used in swim training to build leg strength, improve ankle flexibility, and help swimmers maintain proper body position.
The 50-meter freestyle, nicknamed 'the splash and dash,' is the shortest race in Olympic swimming and typically takes around 21 seconds for men.
Tom Daley and Matty Lee won synchronized 10m platform gold for Great Britain at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
Dry-land training for swimmers includes exercises like resistance bands, medicine ball throws, and core stability work to build power outside the pool.
The backstroke flip turn requires swimmers to transition to their front before executing the flip, then push off the wall on their back.
Sun Yang of China became the first male swimmer to win the 200m, 400m, 800m, and 1500m freestyle world titles over his career.
In water polo, a player who commits a major foul is excluded from the game for 20 seconds, similar to a penalty box in hockey.
The 'catch' phase of the freestyle stroke is the moment when the hand enters the water and begins pulling — it's considered the most technically important part of the stroke.
Infinity pools create a visual effect of water extending to the horizon by having one or more edges where water flows over into a catch basin below.
Swimming was part of the first modern Olympics in 1896, with only men competing in four events in open water.
The modern flip turn was popularized in the 1950s and allows freestyle and backstroke swimmers to reverse direction without touching the wall with their hands.
Greg Louganis is widely considered the greatest diver in history, winning gold medals in both springboard and platform at the 1984 and 1988 Olympics.
An Olympic diving pool is usually kept at around 26°C (79°F), slightly warmer than the competition swimming pool, to prevent muscle cramping.
Michael Phelps won eight gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, surpassing Mark Spitz's record of seven.
The term 'negative split' refers to swimming the second half of a race faster than the first half — a strategy favored by distance swimmers.
Artistic swimming routines are judged on execution, artistic impression, and difficulty, much like figure skating.
Lane ropes in competitive pools are designed with small rotating discs that absorb waves and reduce turbulence between lanes.
Missy Franklin won four gold medals at the 2012 London Olympics at age 17, competing in backstroke and relay events.
The San Alfonso del Mar pool in Chile was once the world's largest swimming pool at over 1,000 meters long, holding 250 million liters of seawater.
Breaststroke swimmers must touch the wall with both hands simultaneously at each turn and finish, a rule strictly enforced by officials.
The Alcatraz swim, crossing San Francisco Bay from the former prison island, covers about 1.5 miles of cold, current-swept water.
FINA, the international governing body for aquatic sports, was founded in 1908 and rebranded as World Aquatics in 2023.
In platform diving, athletes can reach speeds of up to 55 km/h (34 mph) when entering the water from the 10-meter platform.
The 'streamline' position — arms extended overhead, hands stacked, body tight — is used off every wall to minimize drag and maximize distance underwater.
Lap counters are held up poolside to show distance swimmers how many lengths they have remaining during long races like the 1500m.
Ryan Lochte's signature backstroke-to-breaststroke turn, named the 'Lochte Rule,' involved flipping onto the stomach early and was eventually addressed by officials.
Women's swimming was added to the Olympics in 1912 at the Stockholm Games, with events in 100m freestyle and the 4×100m relay.
Swimming burns roughly 400 to 700 calories per hour depending on intensity and stroke, making it one of the most efficient full-body workouts.
The 'I.M. order' — butterfly, backstroke, breaststroke, freestyle — is reversed in medley relays, where backstroke goes first so the swimmer can start in the water.
Synchronized diving was introduced at the 2000 Sydney Olympics, featuring pairs of divers performing identical dives simultaneously.
A swimmer's 'feel for the water' refers to the proprioceptive sense of how their hands and arms interact with the water's resistance.
The famous Bondi Icebergs pool in Sydney, Australia, is built into the rocks at the south end of Bondi Beach and is regularly swamped by ocean waves.
Caeleb Dressel holds the world record in the 100m butterfly with a time of 49.45 seconds, set at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
The earliest known competitive swimming races took place in Japan in 36 BC, according to historical records.
In water polo, the 'eggbeater kick' allows players to maintain a stable, elevated position in the water while keeping their hands free for passing and shooting.
Training paddles are plastic hand paddles that increase the surface area of the pull, building upper-body strength and refining stroke mechanics.
The butterfly stroke requires simultaneous arm movement and a dolphin kick, making it widely considered the most physically demanding stroke.
Pieter van den Hoogenband of the Netherlands upset Ian Thorpe to win the 100m freestyle gold at the 2000 Sydney Olympics.
Susie O'Neill of Australia, known as 'Madame Butterfly,' held the 200m butterfly world record for over a decade.
The first swimming goggles designed for competitive use appeared in the 1930s, but they weren't permitted in Olympic competition until 1976.
A swimmer performing a diving start can enter the water at speeds exceeding 8 meters per second, making the start critical in sprint events.
The Dead Sea, at over 30% salinity, is so buoyant that swimming in the traditional sense is nearly impossible — you simply float.
Tom Daley learned to dive at the age of seven and was competing nationally by age nine.
In artistic swimming, underwater speakers allow athletes to hear the music while submerged, keeping their movements in sync.
The 'gallop' entry in diving refers to a running approach on the platform, used for forward-facing dives from the 10-meter height.
Distance swimmers consume liquid carbohydrate gels and drinks during open water races, grabbing them from feeding stations on poles.
Natalie Coughlin became the first woman to swim the 100m backstroke in under a minute, clocking 59.58 seconds in 2002.
An Olympic pool holds approximately 2.5 million liters (660,000 gallons) of water.
The 'high elbow catch' is a freestyle technique where the elbow stays near the surface while the forearm and hand sweep downward to maximize propulsion.
Sarah Sjöström of Sweden is known for having one of the most powerful butterfly strokes in women's swimming history.
Water polo goals measure 3 meters wide and 90 centimeters high, and goalkeepers are the only players who can use both hands on the ball.
The world record for the longest continuous swim is held by Martin Strel, who swam the entire length of the Amazon River — 5,268 km — in 66 days in 2007.
Hypoxic training involves restricting breathing to every 5, 7, or 9 strokes to build lung capacity and comfort with oxygen debt.
The Federación Internacional de Natación was founded on July 19, 1908, during the London Olympics.
Flip turns generate more speed than open turns because the swimmer never stops moving and pushes off the wall in a streamlined position.
Adam Peaty of Great Britain dominated the 100m breaststroke, becoming the first swimmer to break 57 seconds in the event.
The swimsuit evolved from heavy wool garments in the early 1900s to nylon in the 1960s and high-tech materials like polyurethane by the 2000s.
Diving difficulty ratings range from 1.0 to over 4.0 — a dive's final score is calculated by multiplying the judges' scores by the degree of difficulty.
The 'catch-up drill' in freestyle has swimmers complete one full arm stroke before the other arm begins, emphasizing glide and body rotation.
Janet Evans won three gold medals at the 1988 Seoul Olympics in distance freestyle events, setting world records that stood for years.
In open water swimming, jellyfish stings, cold water, and strong currents are among the most common hazards athletes face.
A standard water polo game consists of four 8-minute quarters, with a 30-second shot clock for each possession.
César Cielo of Brazil holds the world record in the 50m freestyle at 20.91 seconds, set in a tech suit era in 2009.
Silicone swim caps are preferred over latex by many swimmers because they are more durable, don't pull hair as much, and create less drag.
The 'underwater phase' after a start or turn can last up to 15 meters — FINA rules require the swimmer's head to break the surface before the 15-meter mark.
Guo Jingjing of China is considered the greatest female springboard diver in history, winning four Olympic gold medals.
Bilateral breathing — breathing to both sides — helps swimmers develop symmetrical strokes and better awareness of competitors in adjacent lanes.
The ancient Olympic Games in Greece did not include swimming as an event, despite the Greeks being skilled swimmers.
Shane Gould of Australia won three individual gold medals at the 1972 Munich Olympics at age 15, then retired from competitive swimming at 16.
In a medley relay, four different swimmers each swim one stroke: backstroke, breaststroke, butterfly, and freestyle, in that order.
The 'pike' position in diving requires the body to be bent at the hips with legs straight and toes pointed, forming a V shape.
Resistance bands are a staple of dry-land training for swimmers, simulating the pulling motion of strokes to build sport-specific strength.
Australia's 4×100m freestyle relay team, known as the 'Thorpedo relay,' won a dramatic gold at the 2000 Sydney Olympics.
The first heated swimming pool is believed to have been built by Gaius Maecenas in Rome in the 1st century BC.
In artistic swimming, athletes use nose clips to prevent water from entering their nostrils during inverted positions.
The 'tuck' position in diving has the body curled tightly with knees drawn to the chest, allowing for faster rotation.
Caeleb Dressel's 100m freestyle world record of 46.96 seconds was set at the 2021 ISL in Budapest.
Swimmers shave their bodies before major competitions to reduce drag and increase tactile sensitivity in the water.
The first swimming strokes taught to beginners are typically freestyle and backstroke because of their natural body positions and simple breathing patterns.
Duke Kahanamoku, a Hawaiian swimmer and Olympic gold medalist, is also credited with popularizing the sport of surfing worldwide.
In Olympic diving, seven judges score each dive from 0 to 10 in half-point increments, with the two highest and two lowest scores discarded.
The 'descend set' is a training method where each successive repeat is swum faster than the previous one, building pace awareness.
Penny Heyns of South Africa won both the 100m and 200m breaststroke gold medals at the 1996 Atlanta Olympics.
Olympic swimming pools have a minimum depth of 2 meters (6.6 feet) to minimize wave reflection from the bottom.
The 'taper' period typically lasts 2 to 3 weeks before a major championship, during which training volume drops significantly while intensity remains high.
Synchronized diving scores factor in both individual execution and how closely the two divers match each other's timing and form.
Lynne Cox swam across the Bering Strait from Alaska to the Soviet Union in 1987, a distance of 2.7 miles in 3.6°C (38°F) water.
The 'sculling' motion with the hands is fundamental to all strokes — it's the figure-eight pattern that generates continuous propulsion.
An average competitive swimmer performs about 1 million strokes per year during training.
Water temperature in Olympic competition pools is maintained between 25°C and 28°C (77°F to 82°F).
Michael Phelps competed in five consecutive Olympics from 2000 to 2016, making his final Games in Rio de Janeiro at age 31.
The 'layout' or 'straight' position in diving requires the body to be fully extended with no bend at the hips or knees.
Butterfly was officially recognized as a separate stroke by FINA in 1952 after years of swimmers using it in breaststroke events.
Wu Minxia of China competed in five consecutive Olympics in diving, winning five gold medals over her career.
Ankle flexibility is crucial for an efficient kick — many elite swimmers can point their toes almost flat, like a ballerina.
The short course world records are generally faster than long course because swimmers benefit from more turns and more time in streamline off walls.
In water polo, 'wet passes' are deliberately thrown into the water to a teammate's advantage, making it easier to catch while treading water.
The YMCA played a major role in promoting swimming in America, building public pools and offering swim classes starting in the late 1800s.
Kristof Milák of Hungary set the 200m butterfly world record at 1:50.73 at the 2019 World Championships, breaking Phelps's long-standing record.
Controlled breathing in swimming differs from most sports — exhaling occurs underwater and inhaling is timed with head rotation or lifting.
The 'Tarzan drill' involves swimming freestyle with the head completely above water, strengthening the neck and building awareness of sighting in open water.
Ryan Murphy won the backstroke double (100m and 200m) at the 2016 Rio Olympics.
In the 'free' position in diving, the diver can combine tuck, pike, or straight positions during different phases of the dive.
The Marina Bay Sands infinity pool in Singapore, perched 57 stories above ground, is one of the most photographed pools in the world.
Ancient Roman aqueducts supplied water to public swimming baths called 'thermae,' which were central gathering places in Roman cities.
Speedo developed the Fastskin suit by studying sharkskin's dermal denticles, which reduce hydrodynamic drag.
Rowdy Gaines won three gold medals at the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics and later became one of swimming's most recognizable TV commentators.
The 'breakout' — the transition from underwater to surface swimming after a start or turn — is one of the most practiced skills in competitive swimming.
In artistic swimming, teams are penalized if any athlete touches the bottom of the pool during the routine.
A swimmer's stroke rate is measured in cycles per minute, with sprinters typically taking more strokes per minute than distance swimmers.
The world record for the fastest English Channel crossing is 6 hours and 55 minutes, set by Trent Grimsey of Australia in 2012.
Cody Miller, a 2016 Olympic breaststroke medalist, was born with pectus excavatum, a condition where the breastbone is sunken into the chest.
Vertical kicking drills, where swimmers kick while in a vertical position with arms above water, are used to build powerful kick endurance.
The 4×100m medley relay is traditionally the last swimming event at the Olympics, serving as a grand finale.
In diving, 'balking' is when a diver starts their approach but stops or restarts — it results in a 2-point deduction from each judge.
Dara Torres competed in five Olympic Games spanning 24 years, winning her last medal at age 41 in 2008.
The 'catch-up' position in freestyle ensures full extension and glide before the next stroke cycle begins.
Clara Lamore pioneered artistic swimming in the early 1900s, performing synchronized routines at the 1907 New York Hippodrome.
In water polo, a 'dry pass' is thrown through the air directly to a teammate's hand, allowing immediate shots or passes.
Olympic pools use an electronic starting system called the Omega Quantum Timer that simultaneously sends a sound to speakers behind each block for fairness.
The 1500m freestyle, known as 'the mile' despite being slightly shorter, is the longest individual pool event in Olympic competition.
Katie Ledecky's 1500m freestyle world record of 15:20.48 was set at the 2018 TYR Pro Swim Series.
Snorkels used in swim training isolate the stroke by eliminating the need to turn the head to breathe, improving body alignment.
The 'recovery' phase of a swim stroke refers to the part where the arm exits the water and returns forward for the next pull.
Fu Mingxia of China won her first Olympic diving gold medal at age 13 in 1992 and went on to win four Olympic golds total.
Lane assignments in Olympic finals are based on semifinal times, with the fastest swimmer placed in lane 4 (center).
Chloramine, not chlorine itself, is what causes the strong 'pool smell' — it's formed when chlorine reacts with nitrogen compounds from sweat and urine.
The 'pace clock' is a large poolside clock with second and minute hands used by swimmers and coaches to time intervals during training.
Aaron Peirsol won five Olympic gold medals in backstroke events between 2000 and 2008.
In diving, the 'armstand' position has the diver beginning the dive from a handstand on the edge of the platform — it's exclusively a platform dive.
Open water swimming events use a mass start format, with all swimmers beginning simultaneously, leading to physical jostling at the start.
The world's oldest swimming club, the Arlington Baths Club in Glasgow, Scotland, was founded in 1870.
A typical collegiate swimmer covers 6,000 to 12,000 meters per day in practice, depending on their specialty and the phase of the season.
Kosuke Kitajima of Japan won breaststroke gold in both the 100m and 200m at the 2004 Athens Olympics and repeated the feat in 2008.
The Ederle-Gonzalez technique of head-up breaststroke is commonly used in lifeguard training for approaching a distressed swimmer.
Fruity-scented swim caps became popular with recreational swimmers in the 2010s but offer no competitive advantage.
The 'underwater dolphin kick' is so effective that it's sometimes called 'the fifth stroke' — it's used by all strokes coming off the walls.
In water polo, the cap with the number 1 and a different color (usually red for one team, blue for the other) always designates the goalkeeper.
Depression and anxiety are common among elite swimmers after major competitions, a phenomenon sometimes called 'post-Olympic blues.'
The butterfly kick in backstroke swimming (done underwater off walls) can generate more speed than surface backstroke swimming.
Leisel Jones of Australia won an Olympic medal in breaststroke at three consecutive Games: 2000, 2004, and 2008.
Diving judges evaluate five components: approach, takeoff, elevation, execution of movements, and entry into the water.
Paddles come in various shapes — some emphasize fingertip pressure, others power, and some are specifically designed to improve stroke technique.
The first Olympic diving events took place at the 1904 St. Louis Games, featuring only platform diving.
Long-distance open water swimmers use a technique called 'sighting' — briefly lifting the head every few strokes to check navigation landmarks.
The 'broken swim' is a training technique where a race distance is broken into segments with short rest between, simulating race pace.
Matt Biondi won seven medals (five gold) at the 1988 Seoul Olympics, excelling in sprint freestyle and relay events.
The 'degree of difficulty' in diving is calculated based on the number of somersaults, twists, body position, and approach used.
Corking — accidentally entering the water short of completing a rotation — is one of the most common errors in difficult platform dives.
The 'bilateral' breathing pattern alternates breathing sides every three strokes and is considered fundamental to balanced freestyle.
Inge de Bruijn of the Netherlands won four Olympic gold medals in sprint freestyle and butterfly at the 2000 and 2004 Games.
The Wuhan Water Sports Center pool in China uses an advanced wave-dampening system that allows nearly zero wake during competitions.
In artistic swimming, gelatin is applied to hair to keep it rigidly in place during routines — it's practically a second swim cap.
Kicking accounts for only about 10-15% of propulsion in freestyle but is essential for body position and balance.
The swimming events at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics were held at the Tokyo Aquatics Centre, a venue with a capacity of 15,000.
The 'power rack' is used in dry-land training to help swimmers build explosive starts and turns through squats and deadlifts.
Brendan Hansen held multiple world records in breaststroke and won Olympic gold in the 4×100m medley relay in 2004.
The 10-meter platform in diving has a surface area of at least 6 meters long and 2 meters wide.
In water polo, the 'hole set' is the offensive position closest to the goal, often occupied by the strongest and most physical player.
Swimmers can lose up to 1 liter of sweat per hour in warm pools, making hydration important even while immersed in water.
Alexandre Despatie of Canada was one of the youngest-ever World Championship diving gold medalists, winning the 10m platform at age 13.
The 'Tarzan' freestyle drill is named after the movie character and is used to develop open-water sighting skills.
Michael Phelps's final individual Olympic race was the 200m individual medley at the 2016 Rio Games, which he won for the third consecutive time.
Breaststroke timing is critical — the pull and kick must be coordinated so they don't overlap, or the swimmer loses propulsion.
The Melbourne Cricket Ground swimming pool was the venue for the 1956 Olympics, which saw Dawn Fraser emerge as a global star.
Ultraviolet light pool sanitization systems are increasingly used alongside chlorine to reduce chemical irritation and smell.
A swimmer's 'drag coefficient' is influenced by body shape, swimsuit, body position, and surface turbulence.
In diving, the 'inward' group consists of dives where the diver faces the board and rotates toward the board — among the most counterintuitive to learn.
The U.S. water polo men's team won their first Olympic medal (silver) in over 20 years at the 2008 Beijing Olympics.
Open water swimmers in cold conditions may experience 'cold water shock,' an involuntary gasp reflex that can cause drowning if the face is submerged.
A typical backstroke race begins with swimmers gripping a device called a 'backstroke ledge' or 'starting wedge' mounted on the wall below the blocks.
Katie Ledecky has won more individual Olympic and World Championship gold medals in women's swimming than any other swimmer in history.
The Pan Pacific Championships and the Mare Nostrum series are two of the most prestigious non-Olympic swimming competitions.
Samantha Riley of Australia set multiple breaststroke world records in the 1990s.
In synchronized diving, the two divers must perform the same dive but can take off from different positions (one can stand, one can do an armstand in platform).
The 'T-pace' in swim training refers to the pace a swimmer can maintain over a 1,000-yard time trial, used as a baseline for training sets.
Hironoshin Furuhashi of Japan broke multiple world freestyle records in the late 1940s, earning the nickname 'the Flying Fish of Fujiyama.'
The first women's Olympic diving events were held at the 1912 Stockholm Games, with only a plain high dive from the platform.
Chlorine levels in competitive pools are strictly monitored and must stay between 1 and 3 parts per million.
The 'gallop step' approach in platform diving allows the diver to build forward momentum while maintaining control before takeoff.
Federica Pellegrini of Italy set multiple world records in the 200m freestyle and competed in five consecutive Olympics.
The 'gutter' system around the perimeter of a modern pool channels displaced water away, reducing wave action and turbulence.
In water polo, a penalty throw is awarded for a major foul inside the 6-meter line and is taken from the 5-meter line with only the goalkeeper defending.
The backstroke start is the only start in competitive swimming performed from the water, with swimmers pushing off the wall on a signal.
At the 1936 Berlin Olympics, Japanese swimmers won a combined total of 11 medals, dominating the men's swimming events.
The 'kick-on-side' drill trains swimmers to maintain a streamlined, rotated body position while developing an efficient flutter kick.
Greg Louganis scored a perfect 10 from all seven judges on a reverse 3½ somersault tuck at the 1984 Olympics.
Cate Campbell and Bronte Campbell of Australia are one of the most successful sibling pairs in Olympic swimming history.
In Olympic open water swimming, water temperature must be at least 16°C (61°F) for the event to proceed.
The 'DPS' (distance per stroke) drill emphasizes taking fewer, more powerful strokes to cover the same distance, building efficiency.
Dave McCagg invented the anti-wave lane rope in 1961, revolutionizing competitive swimming by reducing cross-lane turbulence.
The Texas Swimming Center, home to the University of Texas Longhorns, is one of the premier college swimming facilities in the United States.
In artistic swimming, teams can consist of duets, trios, or teams of 4-8 athletes performing synchronized routines.
Grant Hackett of Australia dominated the 1500m freestyle, winning the world title four times and Olympic gold in 2000 and silver in 2004.
Fatigue-resistant 'Type I' slow-twitch muscle fibers are predominant in distance swimmers, while sprinters rely more on 'Type II' fast-twitch fibers.
An incoming diver hits the water with a force equivalent to about 1.5 times their body weight when diving from 10 meters.
The 'sculling' drill has swimmers lying on their backs or fronts, using only small hand movements to propel themselves, building water feel.
Stephanie Rice of Australia won three gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics in the 200m IM, 400m IM, and medley relay.
In diving, the 'reverse' group involves takeoff from a forward-facing position with backward rotation — away from the board.
Nose clips in artistic swimming are custom-fitted to each athlete to ensure a secure seal during inverted underwater positions.
The concept of 'feel for the water' is often cited by coaches as the hardest skill to teach and the most important to master.
The 800m freestyle was historically only an event for women at the Olympics, while men swam the 1500m — both genders now compete in both distances.
Swimming is one of the most-watched sports during the Summer Olympics, regularly drawing television audiences in the hundreds of millions.
Dimitri Sautin of Russia competed in diving at six consecutive Olympics from 1992 to 2012, winning two medals.
The 'drag suit' is an oversized, loose-fitting suit worn over a regular swimsuit during training to increase resistance and build strength.
In breaststroke, the pullout — one underwater pull and dolphin kick off each wall — is the only time a butterfly kick is allowed in the stroke.
Kieren Perkins of Australia won the 1500m freestyle gold at the 1992 Barcelona Olympics and repeated the feat at the 1996 Atlanta Games from lane 8.
Body rotation in freestyle should be about 45-60 degrees to each side, driven by the hips, not the shoulders.
The 'negative split strategy' in the 400m IM involves conserving energy in the butterfly and backstroke legs to attack the breaststroke and freestyle legs.
In the early 1900s, women were required to wear swimming costumes that covered them from neck to knee — violations could result in arrest.
Pool temperature affects performance: water that's too warm reduces the body's ability to dissipate heat, leading to faster fatigue.
The 'twist' dives in competitive diving involve rotation around the body's vertical axis in addition to somersault rotations.
Ariarne Titmus of Australia defeated Katie Ledecky to win the 400m freestyle gold at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
The backstroke flags are hung 5 meters from each end of the pool to alert swimmers that the wall is approaching.
In water polo, goalkeepers can use both hands, punch the ball with a clenched fist, and touch the ball with both hands within the 5-meter area.
Vladimir Salnikov of the Soviet Union was the first swimmer to break the 15-minute barrier in the 1500m freestyle, swimming 14:58.27 in 1980.
The 'catch phase' in butterfly involves both arms entering the water simultaneously and sweeping outward before pulling back.
Race analysis technology now tracks swimmers' stroke count, tempo, split times, and underwater distance in real time during competitions.
The first swimming pool in the United States was built in Brookline, Massachusetts, in 1887.
The 'frog kick' in breaststroke involves bending the knees, rotating the feet outward, and sweeping them back together in a circular motion.
Pan Zhanle of China broke the 100m freestyle world record with a time of 46.40 seconds at the 2024 Paris Olympics.
Swimmer's shoulder is a common overuse injury caused by the repetitive overhead motion of strokes, particularly freestyle and butterfly.
In diving, a 'failed dive' is scored 0 when the diver performs a dive different from the one announced.
The 100m freestyle is often called 'the blue riband event' of swimming, akin to the 100m dash in track and field.
Mary T. Meagher, known as 'Madame Butterfly,' set 200m butterfly world records in 1981 that stood for nearly 20 years.
Anti-fog spray or solutions are commonly applied to the inside of swim goggles to maintain clear vision throughout a swim.
The 'Lochte rule' led to FINA clarifying that backstroke swimmers must be on their back when they leave the wall in medley events.
Open water swimming navigation is so challenging that some swimmers finish hundreds of meters over the actual course distance.
The first World Aquatics Championships were held in Belgrade, Yugoslavia, in 1973.
Creatine and beta-alanine are among the most researched supplements used by competitive swimmers to improve sprint performance and delay fatigue.
Simone Manuel became the first African American woman to win an individual Olympic gold medal in swimming at the 2016 Rio Games.
The 'Power Tower' is a training device that provides variable resistance as a swimmer is tethered and pulled back to the wall.
In platform diving, divers often use a shammy cloth to dry off between dives, as a wet body changes how they grip their legs in tuck positions.
Mixed-gender relays were introduced at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, adding a new tactical dimension to relay swimming.
Matt Zubalik holds the Guinness World Record for the most continuous hours swimming, at 63 hours and 39 minutes.
The 'finis tempo trainer' is a small electronic metronome worn under a swim cap that beeps at a set frequency, helping swimmers maintain a target stroke rate.
Chad le Clos of South Africa upset Michael Phelps to win the 200m butterfly gold at the 2012 London Olympics.
In water polo, the 'pressing defense' is a full-pool defensive strategy that tries to intercept passes and disrupt the opponent's offensive setup.
The two-beat kick uses one kick per arm stroke and is favored by distance swimmers for its energy efficiency.
The six-beat kick uses three kicks per arm stroke (six per full cycle) and is common among sprinters for maximum propulsion.
Platform divers develop 'air awareness' — the ability to know exactly where they are in space during a dive — through thousands of repetitions.
Rebecca Adlington of Great Britain won two gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, including the 800m freestyle.
The 'pull' phase of the stroke is when the hand and forearm generate the most propulsion, moving water backward to propel the swimmer forward.
In artistic swimming, routines last between 2 and 4 minutes, and athletes may spend up to 50% of that time underwater.
The 'warm-down pool' at major competitions allows swimmers to recover immediately after racing by swimming at low intensity.
Ian Crocker's 100m butterfly world record of 50.40, set in 2005, was the benchmark for nearly a decade.
Some marathon swimmers train in ice baths and cold lakes to acclimatize their bodies to the frigid temperatures they may encounter in open water.
The 'eggbeater kick' used in water polo and artistic swimming involves alternating circular kicks that keep the torso stable above water.
Cullen Jones became one of the first African American swimmers to earn an Olympic gold medal, winning the 4×100m freestyle relay in 2008.
In diving, a 'balk' on the springboard results in a mandatory deduction; two balks in the same dive result in a failed dive (zero score).
The 'threshold set' in training targets the anaerobic threshold — the pace just below where lactate accumulation overwhelms the muscles.
Yusra Mardini, a Syrian refugee, swam at the 2016 and 2020 Olympics as part of the Refugee Olympic Team after swimming for her life across the Aegean Sea.
The 'over-distance' training approach has swimmers covering distances longer than their race distance at moderate pace to build aerobic endurance.
Jenny Thompson won 12 Olympic medals (8 gold) across four Games, all in relay events — the most by any female swimmer in U.S. history.
In water polo, the 'counterattack' is a fast break initiated immediately after gaining possession, aiming to score before the defense can set up.
The London Aquatics Centre, designed by Zaha Hadid for the 2012 Olympics, features a wave-shaped roof that has become an architectural icon.
Fingertip paddles are small paddles that attach only to the fingers, emphasizing the catch and early vertical forearm position.
The 'IM switch' — transitioning smoothly between strokes in the individual medley — is a practiced skill that can gain or lose significant time.
David Wilkie of Great Britain won the 200m breaststroke gold medal at the 1976 Montreal Olympics.
In the 2008 Olympics, the U.S. 4×100m freestyle relay staged one of the greatest comeback victories in Olympic history, with Jason Lezak anchoring.
The 'snorkel drill' removes the breathing variable from the stroke, allowing swimmers to focus entirely on body position and pull mechanics.
A typical taper for an Olympic swimmer involves reducing yardage from around 10,000 meters per day to about 3,000-4,000 meters.
The 'catch-up drill' is one of the most universally prescribed freestyle drills for developing a long, efficient stroke.
Libby Trickett of Australia won four Olympic medals and held the 100m freestyle world record in a tech suit.
In artistic swimming, the 'boost' or 'throw' involves teammates launching one swimmer high out of the water for acrobatic movements.
The term 'heat sheet' refers to the printed listing of all swimmers entered in each event at a swim meet, including their seed times and lane assignments.
Pieter van den Hoogenband's 100m freestyle gold at the 2000 Sydney Olympics came with a world record of 47.84 seconds.
Breath control in breaststroke is unique — the head must break the surface on every stroke, unlike butterfly where some coaches teach breathing every other stroke.
Electronic touchpads at the ends of competitive pools are connected to timing systems accurate to 1/1000th of a second, though official results are given to 1/100th.
The 'anchor leg' of a relay is usually swum by the team's fastest or most experienced swimmer, as they can respond to the race situation.
The world's largest swimming competition by number of participants is the Midmar Mile in South Africa, which attracts over 20,000 swimmers annually.
In diving, each diver must submit a list of planned dives before the competition, and they cannot change them once the event begins.
The Lia Neal Story inspired many young African American swimmers after she became the youngest member of the 2012 U.S. Olympic swim team at age 17.
The 'pace line' is a training formation where swimmers follow each other closely in a lane, drafting off the swimmer ahead to maintain faster paces.
Ranomi Kromowidjojo of the Netherlands won the 50m and 100m freestyle golds at the 2012 London Olympics.
The diving well at competition venues is typically separate from the swimming pool and is kept at 5 meters deep to safely absorb divers' entries.
Breast muscles, latissimus dorsi, and deltoids are the primary muscle groups used in the pulling motion of most swimming strokes.
The 'over-the-water recovery' in butterfly requires both arms to clear the water's surface simultaneously.
Lewis Pugh has completed a swim at every major ocean on Earth, including a 1 km swim in the Arctic at the North Pole.
The split time is the recorded time for each individual length in a race, used by coaches and analysts to assess pacing strategy.
In water polo, the ball weighs between 400 and 450 grams and has a circumference of 68-71 cm for men and 65-67 cm for women.
The 'touch-and-go' turn is used in breaststroke and butterfly — swimmers must touch the wall with both hands before pushing off.
World Aquatics (formerly FINA) recognized short course meters (25m pools) records separately from long course (50m pools) records starting in 1991.
The Romanian women's water polo team, once little-known, has risen in prominence on the international stage in recent years.
A typical competition warm-up pool is heated to around 27-28°C and is available for swimmers to use before and between events.
Michael Phelps's career spanned from 2000 to 2016 and included 83 medals in major international competitions — more than any other swimmer in history.
In diving, the judges sit on either side of the pool at water level to observe the dive from multiple angles.
The 'feel drill' involves swimming with closed fists, forcing the swimmer to use the forearm for propulsion and enhancing water sensitivity.
Some Olympic pools use a standing-wave generator at one end to create consistent flow and prevent dead water lanes.
The International Swimming Hall of Fame is located in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, and honors the greatest swimmers, divers, and water polo players in history.
Takeshi Matsuda of Japan competed in four consecutive Olympics, winning a silver in the 200m butterfly at the 2012 London Games.
The 'buoy turn' in open water swimming refers to turning around a marker buoy placed along the course, requiring good sighting skills.
Jodie Henry of Australia won the 100m freestyle gold at the 2004 Athens Olympics and helped Australia to relay gold.
In synchronized diving, judges score both the execution of individual dives and the synchronization between the pair.
The 'build swim' is a training tool where the swimmer progressively increases speed throughout the repeat, finishing at near-maximum effort.
A 'negative split' in the 200m butterfly might involve a first 100m in 55 seconds and a second 100m in 54 seconds.
Modern starting blocks in swimming are angled and include an adjustable back plate that allows swimmers to push off with both feet for explosive starts.
The 'track start' position, with one foot forward and one back on the block, is the most common starting technique in modern competitive swimming.
In water polo, teams consist of six field players and one goalkeeper in the water, with up to six substitutes on the bench.
Sarah Sjöström became the first woman to break 56 seconds in the 100m butterfly, setting a world record at the 2016 Rio Olympics.
The 'popov pull' — named after Alexander Popov — emphasizes a relaxed, high-elbow recovery and a powerful catch in sprint freestyle.
In artistic swimming, highlights of the scoring include a 'degree of difficulty' component for acrobatic and hybrid elements.
Flip-turn specialists can gain up to half a second per wall compared to swimmers with weaker turns — in a distance race, this advantage multiplies.
Anthony Ervin won gold in the 50m freestyle at the 2000 Sydney Olympics and won it again 16 years later at the 2016 Rio Olympics, aged 35.
The 'stretch cordz' or 'surgical tubing' attached to a belt around the waist provides resistance that swimmers must work against in the pool.
In the early 1800s, competitive swimming events in England were held in natural bodies of water like the Thames River.
The 200m backstroke is considered one of the most demanding individual events because it requires sustained speed with limited oxygen access.
Diving bubbles are generated by a machine beneath the pool surface to break surface tension, giving divers a visual reference for the water surface from height.
The 'grind set' in training is a high-volume, moderate-intensity workout designed to build aerobic base and mental toughness.
Ye Shiwen of China won the 200m and 400m individual medley golds at the 2012 London Olympics at age 16.
In water polo, the 'exclusion area' is the zone behind the goal line where excluded players must wait out their 20-second penalty.
The 'power phase' of the stroke begins at the catch and ends when the hand exits the water near the hip.
Katinka Hosszú of Hungary won three individual gold medals at the 2016 Rio Olympics, earning the nickname 'Iron Lady.'
The 'superman drill' in freestyle has the swimmer fully extending both arms forward with the face down, kicking, and taking a stroke only on the coach's command.
Competitive swimming uses a false start rule where a single false start results in disqualification — there are no second chances.
Penny Oleksiak became the youngest Olympic champion in Canadian history when she won gold in the 100m freestyle at age 16 at the 2016 Rio Olympics.
The 'threshold pace' or 'CSS' (critical swim speed) is a training metric representing the fastest pace a swimmer can sustain without accumulating excess lactate.
In diving, the 'twisting' group includes dives with at least one twist combined with somersaults — they have some of the highest degrees of difficulty.
Pool lane dividers evolved from simple ropes in the early 1900s to the sophisticated anti-wave lane lines used today.
Swimming is recommended by most medical professionals as a low-impact exercise suitable for people with joint problems, arthritis, and rehabilitation needs.
The 'test set' is a benchmark workout — such as 5×200m freestyle on a specified interval — that coaches use to gauge a swimmer's fitness level throughout the season.
In the relay exchange, the outgoing swimmer must not leave the block until the incoming swimmer has touched the wall — false starts result in disqualification.
The photo finish camera in competitive swimming captures 100 images per second to help determine results in extremely close races.
Xu Haifeng won China's first-ever Olympic gold in any sport (shooting) in 1984, but China's aquatic dominance grew through diving in the decades following.
Nathan Adrian won gold in the 100m freestyle at the 2012 London Olympics by just 0.01 seconds over James Magnussen.
The 'fly-kick' drill in breaststroke involves adding a single dolphin kick during the underwater pullout to practice the legal underwater phase.
In artistic swimming, the 'rocket split' is an acrobatic lift where a swimmer is launched out of the water and performs a split position in the air.
Michael Phelps's first Olympic gold came in the 400m individual medley at the 2004 Athens Olympics.
The 'descend 1-3' training instruction means the first repeat is slowest and the third is fastest, teaching pace control.
At the 1976 Montreal Olympics, the East German women's swimming team won 11 of 13 gold medals, later revealed to be aided by a state-sponsored doping program.
A 'dead spot' in a swimmer's stroke is any point where propulsion temporarily stops, creating deceleration — great swimmers minimize dead spots.
The 'Omega Quantum Aquatics Timer' used at the Olympics can measure times to 1/1000th of a second through touchpad sensitivity.
In water polo, the shot clock (30 seconds) resets when the attacking team earns an exclusion foul or corner throw.
The classic 'race dive' in swimming involves a flat, streamlined entry with arms extended forward and the body nearly horizontal to minimize depth and maximize distance.
Backstroke swimmers often struggle with swimming in straight lines because they cannot see the lane line below them.
The Aquatics Centre at Olympic Park London used over 3,000 tons of steel and 160,000 tiles in its construction.
David Boudia won 10m platform diving gold for the U.S. at the 2012 London Olympics.
In the 1900 Paris Olympics, swimming events included an obstacle race and an underwater swimming race — neither was ever held again.
The 'race-pace training' method has swimmers practicing at their goal race pace to develop neuromuscular patterns for competition.
Quan Hongchan of China won the 10m platform diving gold at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics at age 14, earning three perfect 10 scores.
The 'underwater breakout' after a turn is often where races are won or lost — elite swimmers maintain speed off walls better than their competitors.
In breaststroke, the 'out-sweep' and 'in-sweep' of the arms create a heart-shaped pulling pattern that generates propulsion.
The 4×200m freestyle relay is one of the longest relay events in Olympic swimming, often lasting over 7 minutes.
Many elite swimmers practice visualization techniques, mentally rehearsing their races stroke by stroke before competition.
The 'hand entry' in freestyle should be between the shoulder and the midline of the head, with fingers entering first at about a 30-degree angle.
The 1972 Munich Olympics Mark Spitz competition was one of the most watched swimming broadcasts in television history.
An average competitive swimmer's heart rate during a sprint race can exceed 190 beats per minute.
The 'recovery' in backstroke involves the arm exiting pinky-finger first and rotating overhead to re-enter thumb-first.
In water polo, the 'skip shot' bounces off the water surface before entering the goal, making it unpredictable for goalkeepers.
Matt Grevers won gold in the 100m backstroke at the 2012 London Olympics with a time of 52.16 seconds.
The YMCA's 'Learn to Swim' program has taught millions of Americans to swim since its inception in the late 19th century.
Lilly King of the United States won the 100m breaststroke gold at the 2016 Rio Olympics and has been a dominant force in the event.
